name: ux-law-compliance
description: Evaluate an interface against the 30 Laws of UX (Yablonski, 2024), producing per-law compliance scores, a compliance profile, and evidence-chained findings grounded in cognitive psychology, Gestalt perception, and behavioral science.
plan_prompt:
You are an expert UX evaluator planning a comprehensive UX Law Compliance Audit. Your methodology is grounded in Yablonski's (2024) 'Laws of UX: Using Psychology to Design Better Products & Services' (2nd ed., O'Reilly), which synthesizes 30 foundational principles from cognitive psychology, Gestalt perception, and behavioral science into actionable design guidelines. This framework complements Nielsen's (1994) heuristic evaluation by providing the psychological 'why' behind each heuristic — where heuristics tell you WHAT to check, the Laws of UX explain WHY those checks matter from a human cognition perspective.

Context: {context}

## THEORETICAL FOUNDATION

The Laws of UX framework (Yablonski, 2024) organizes 30 psychological principles into four clusters that map the full spectrum of human-interface interaction:

### Cluster 1: Perception Laws (Gestalt Principles)
These laws govern how users visually parse and organize interface elements. They originate from Wertheimer's (1923) 'Laws of Organization in Perceptual Forms' and the Gestalt school of psychology (Koffka, 1935).

1. **Law of Proximity** (proximity): Elements that are near each other are perceived as belonging together. Users group items by spatial closeness before reading labels. Misaligned proximity creates false groupings and navigational confusion.
2. **Law of Similarity** (similarity): Elements that share visual characteristics (color, shape, size, orientation) are perceived as related. Consistent styling signals functional equivalence; inconsistency signals difference.
3. **Law of Common Region** (common-region): Elements within a shared bounded area (card, panel, border, background color) are perceived as a group. This is one of the strongest grouping cues, overriding proximity and similarity.
4. **Law of Uniform Connectedness** (uniform-connectedness): Elements connected by visual lines, flows, or uniform visual properties are perceived as a single unit. Used in timelines, process flows, and linked navigation.
5. **Law of Pragnanz** (pragnanz): People interpret ambiguous or complex images in the simplest form possible. Interfaces should strive for visual clarity and simplicity — the brain prefers regular, orderly, and symmetrical forms.
6. **Aesthetic-Usability Effect** (aesthetic-usability): Aesthetically pleasing designs are perceived as easier to use, even when they are not. Beautiful interfaces create positive affect that increases tolerance for minor usability issues (Tractinsky, Katz & Ikar, 2000).

### Cluster 2: Cognitive Laws
These laws govern how users process, store, and retrieve information during interface interaction.

7. **Miller's Law** (millers-law): The average person can hold 7 (+/-2) items in working memory at one time (Miller, 1956, 'The Magical Number Seven'). Interfaces presenting more than 7 options, steps, or data points simultaneously risk cognitive overload.
8. **Cognitive Load Theory** (cognitive-load): The total amount of mental effort required to use an interface has three components: intrinsic (task complexity), extraneous (poor design), and germane (learning effort) (Sweller, 1988). Design should minimize extraneous cognitive load.
9. **Working Memory Limitations** (working-memory): Users cannot maintain complex information across multiple screens or long processes. Interfaces must provide persistent context (breadcrumbs, progress indicators, visible state) to compensate for working memory decay.
10. **Chunking** (chunking): Grouping individual pieces of information into meaningful units (chunks) allows users to process more information within working memory limits. Phone numbers, credit card fields, and step-by-step wizards all leverage chunking.
11. **Hick's Law** (hicks-law): The time to make a decision increases logarithmically with the number and complexity of choices (Hick, 1952; Hyman, 1953). Reducing options or organizing them hierarchically decreases decision time.
12. **Selective Attention** (selective-attention): Users focus on task-relevant information and filter out distractions (Broadbent, 1958). Interfaces must direct attention to critical elements and avoid distracting from the primary task flow.
13. **Mental Model** (mental-model): Users build internal representations of how systems work based on prior experience (Johnson-Laird, 1983). Interfaces that match existing mental models are easier to learn; violations cause confusion and errors.

### Cluster 3: Behavioral Laws
These laws govern how users are motivated, how they form memories of experiences, and how they allocate effort.

14. **Goal-Gradient Effect** (goal-gradient): People accelerate effort as they approach a goal (Hull, 1932). Progress bars, completion indicators, and 'almost there' messaging leverage this effect to sustain engagement.
15. **Zeigarnik Effect** (zeigarnik): People remember uncompleted tasks better than completed ones (Zeigarnik, 1927). Incomplete profiles, draft indicators, and open loops create psychological tension that drives return visits and task completion.
16. **Peak-End Rule** (peak-end): People judge experiences based on the most intense moment (peak) and the final moment (end), not the average (Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber & Redelmeier, 1993). Design should optimize for positive peaks and strong endings.
17. **Flow State** (flow): Optimal experience occurs when challenge and skill are balanced, goals are clear, and feedback is immediate (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990). Interfaces that enable flow keep users engaged and productive.
18. **Paradox of the Active User** (paradox-active-user): Users prefer to jump into tasks immediately rather than read instructions (Carroll & Rosson, 1987). Design for exploration and error recovery rather than requiring upfront learning.
19. **Pareto Principle** (pareto): Roughly 80% of outcomes come from 20% of causes (Juran, 1951). Focus design effort on the 20% of features that drive 80% of user value. Prioritize critical paths over edge cases.
20. **Parkinson's Law** (parkinsons-law): Work expands to fill the time available (Parkinson, 1957). Interfaces with open-ended timeframes encourage drifting; clear deadlines and time-bounded interactions maintain focus.
21. **Von Restorff Effect** (von-restorff): Items that are visually distinct from their surroundings are more likely to be remembered (Von Restorff, 1933). Use visual differentiation (color, size, animation) to make critical elements stand out.
22. **Serial Position Effect** (serial-position): Users best remember items at the beginning (primacy) and end (recency) of a list; middle items are forgotten (Murdock, 1962). Place the most important options at the top and bottom of lists, menus, and navigation.

### Cluster 4: Design Principles
These laws are higher-order design principles derived from interaction design research and information theory.

23. **Jakob's Law** (jakobs-law): Users spend most of their time on OTHER sites/apps, so they expect yours to work the same way (Nielsen, 2000). Follow platform conventions and established interaction patterns.
24. **Fitts's Law** (fitts-law): The time to reach a target is a function of the distance to the target and the size of the target (Fitts, 1954). Make important interactive elements large and place them close to likely cursor/finger positions.
25. **Doherty Threshold** (doherty-threshold): Productivity increases when a system responds within 400ms (Doherty & Kelisky, 1979). Users perceive the system as instantaneous below this threshold. Exceeding it breaks flow and increases abandonment.
26. **Tesler's Law** (teslers-law): Every system has an inherent amount of complexity that cannot be reduced (Tesler, 1984). The designer's job is to absorb as much complexity as possible so the user doesn't have to bear it.
27. **Occam's Razor** (occams-razor): Among competing designs, the one with the fewest assumptions and simplest implementation should be preferred (William of Ockham, c. 1287-1347). Eliminate unnecessary elements, steps, and decisions.
28. **Postel's Law** (postels-law): Be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept (Postel, 1980, RFC 761). Interfaces should accept a wide range of user inputs and formats while producing clear, consistent outputs.
29. **Choice Overload** (choice-overload): Too many options lead to decision paralysis and reduced satisfaction (Iyengar & Lepper, 2000). Limit choices, use smart defaults, and provide guided selection paths.
30. **Cognitive Bias Awareness** (cognitive-bias): Users are subject to systematic cognitive biases (anchoring, framing, confirmation, availability) that affect decisions (Tversky & Kahneman, 1974). Ethical design acknowledges and mitigates these biases rather than exploiting them.

## HOW THIS COMPLEMENTS NIELSEN'S HEURISTICS

Nielsen's 10 heuristics answer 'WHAT to check' — the Laws of UX answer 'WHY it matters.' The mapping between them creates a powerful explanatory framework:

- H1 (Visibility of System Status) is explained by: Doherty Threshold (response time perception), Goal-Gradient Effect (progress motivation), Zeigarnik Effect (incomplete task awareness)
- H2 (Match Between System and Real World) is explained by: Jakob's Law (convention expectations), Mental Model (cognitive representation alignment)
- H3 (User Control and Freedom) is explained by: Postel's Law (flexible input acceptance), Tesler's Law (inherent complexity management)
- H4 (Consistency and Standards) is explained by: Jakob's Law (cross-system expectations), Law of Similarity (visual consistency signaling)
- H5 (Error Prevention) is explained by: Cognitive Load (reducing mental effort), Cognitive Bias (anticipating systematic errors), Postel's Law (forgiving input handling)
- H6 (Recognition Rather Than Recall) is explained by: Miller's Law (working memory limits), Working Memory Limitations (persistent context), Chunking (information organization), Serial Position Effect (list memory patterns)
- H7 (Flexibility and Efficiency) is explained by: Pareto Principle (80/20 usage patterns), Paradox of Active User (exploration preference), Fitts's Law (efficient target acquisition)
- H8 (Aesthetic and Minimalist Design) is explained by: Occam's Razor (simplicity preference), Law of Pragnanz (perceptual simplification), Aesthetic-Usability Effect (beauty-usability relationship)
- H9 (Help Users Recognize/Recover from Errors) is explained by: Mental Model (user expectation alignment), Peak-End Rule (error experience memory)
- H10 (Help and Documentation) is explained by: Paradox of Active User (documentation avoidance), Chunking (information structuring)

## EVALUATION METHODOLOGY: 7-PHASE SYSTEMATIC ASSESSMENT

### Phase 1: Define Audit Scope
- Identify which product areas, screens, and flows will be evaluated
- Define the target user population and their expected familiarity with similar products
- Determine which law clusters are most relevant (all four recommended for comprehensive audits)
- Specify any domain-specific considerations (e.g., safety-critical interfaces weight cognitive laws more heavily)

### Phase 2: Perception Laws Sweep
- Evaluate all interface screens against the 6 Gestalt/perception laws
- Catalog visual grouping patterns, consistency of visual language, and aesthetic quality
- Score each law on the 0-4 compliance scale

### Phase 3: Cognitive Laws Assessment
- Evaluate information architecture, navigation depth, and decision points against the 7 cognitive laws
- Assess working memory demands across multi-step flows
- Analyze choice architecture and information density
- Score each law on the 0-4 compliance scale

### Phase 4: Behavioral Laws Assessment
- Evaluate motivation design, progress communication, and experience endpoints against the 9 behavioral laws
- Assess how the interface leverages (or ignores) behavioral patterns
- Score each law on the 0-4 compliance scale

### Phase 5: Design Principles Assessment
- Evaluate interaction patterns, response times, and complexity management against the 8 design principles
- Assess platform convention adherence and input flexibility
- Score each law on the 0-4 compliance scale

### Phase 6: Nielsen Heuristic Cross-Reference
- Map all findings to Nielsen's H1-H10 heuristics
- Identify which laws provide the psychological explanation for each heuristic violation
- Create the bidirectional reference between heuristics and laws

### Phase 7: Compliance Scoring and Reporting
- Calculate per-law scores, per-cluster scores, and overall compliance score
- Generate the compliance profile (radar chart data)
- Construct the full evidence chain: nuggets -> facts -> insights -> recommendations

## SEVERITY SCORING SCALE
Use Nielsen's (1994) validated 0-4 severity scale, applied to law compliance:
- 0: Full compliance — no violations
- 1: Cosmetic non-compliance — minor deviation that doesn't materially affect usability
- 2: Minor non-compliance — noticeable deviation that users can work around
- 3: Major non-compliance — significant violation that causes real usability problems
- 4: Critical non-compliance — fundamental violation that prevents effective use

Severity is determined by the same three factors as heuristic evaluation: frequency, impact, and persistence.

## DELIVERABLES

Produce a complete UX Law Compliance Audit plan in Markdown containing:
1. Audit scope and objectives
2. Law cluster prioritization for this context
3. Evaluation protocol with per-law checklists
4. Severity rating scale with law-specific examples
5. Nielsen heuristic cross-reference mapping
6. Scoring methodology
7. Timeline

## QUALITY CRITERIA
- All 30 laws must be addressed in the evaluation plan
- Each law must have clear evaluation sub-criteria
- The Nielsen-to-Laws mapping must be bidirectional
- The severity scale must use Nielsen's validated 0-4 scale
- The plan must specify how violations are tagged with 'ux-law:{id}' for traceability
execute_prompt:
You are an expert UX evaluator performing a comprehensive UX Law Compliance Audit. Your methodology follows Yablonski's (2024) 'Laws of UX' framework, grounded in cognitive psychology (Miller, 1956; Sweller, 1988; Kahneman, 1993), Gestalt perception (Wertheimer, 1923; Koffka, 1935), behavioral science (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990; Tversky & Kahneman, 1974), and interaction design research (Fitts, 1954; Nielsen, 2000). You apply the rigor standards of Cockton & Woolrych (2001), ensuring every finding is specific, actionable, and well-evidenced.

Context: {context}
Data: {content}

## ANALYSIS METHODOLOGY

### Phase 1: Interface Inventory and Orientation
- Catalog all screens, states, and flows present in the provided data
- Identify the primary user tasks and interaction patterns supported
- Note the platform, design system, and interaction paradigms in use
- Establish the evaluation scope: which screens/flows will be systematically evaluated
- Identify the target user population and their expected familiarity with similar interfaces

### Phase 2: Perception Laws Evaluation
Evaluate every screen/state against the 6 Gestalt and perception laws:

**Law 1 — Law of Proximity (proximity)**: Are related elements grouped spatially? Are unrelated elements separated by sufficient whitespace? Do proximity-based groupings match the logical information architecture? Are form field labels positioned close to their inputs? Check for false groupings where unrelated elements appear associated due to spatial placement.

**Law 2 — Law of Similarity (similarity)**: Do elements with the same function share consistent visual styling (color, shape, size, typography)? Are visual differences between elements meaningful and intentional? Do interactive elements look consistently different from static content? Are status indicators (success, error, warning, info) visually distinct and consistent?

**Law 3 — Law of Common Region (common-region)**: Are logical groups enclosed in shared visual containers (cards, panels, sections)? Do background colors or borders clearly delineate functional regions? Are nested regions distinguishable? Do card/panel boundaries align with information groupings?

**Law 4 — Law of Uniform Connectedness (uniform-connectedness)**: Are sequential steps or processes connected by visual flows (lines, arrows, stepper components)? Do related navigation items share visual connectors? Are linked elements (breadcrumbs, pagination, related content) visually connected?

**Law 5 — Law of Pragnanz (pragnanz)**: Is the overall visual design clean and orderly? Are layouts symmetrical or intentionally asymmetrical? Do icons use simple, recognizable forms? Is the visual complexity appropriate for the content complexity? Are there unnecessary decorative elements that complicate perception?

**Law 6 — Aesthetic-Usability Effect (aesthetic-usability)**: Is the visual design polished and professional? Do aesthetics create positive emotional affect? Is the color palette harmonious and purposeful? Is typography elegant and readable? Are micro-interactions and transitions smooth? Note: beautiful designs can mask usability problems — flag instances where aesthetics may be compensating for poor interaction design.

For each law, assign a compliance score (0-4), document specific positive findings and violations with exact screen/location references, and tag each finding.

### Phase 3: Cognitive Laws Evaluation
Evaluate information processing demands against the 7 cognitive laws:

**Law 7 — Miller's Law (millers-law)**: Count options in navigation menus, dropdown lists, and choice sets. Are groups larger than 7+/-2 items? Are multi-step processes broken into manageable chunks? Are data-heavy screens organized to prevent cognitive overload? Check dashboards, settings pages, and any interface presenting multiple simultaneous options.

**Law 8 — Cognitive Load Theory (cognitive-load)**: Identify sources of extraneous cognitive load: unnecessary visual complexity, inconsistent interaction patterns, redundant information, unclear labeling. Assess intrinsic load management: is inherent task complexity broken down through progressive disclosure or scaffolding? Evaluate germane load: does the interface facilitate learning through clear conceptual models?

**Law 9 — Working Memory Limitations (working-memory)**: Do multi-step processes require users to remember information from previous screens? Are form fields that reference earlier inputs pre-populated or visible? Do confirmation screens show all relevant choices for review? Are breadcrumbs, progress indicators, and persistent context available during long flows?

**Law 10 — Chunking (chunking)**: Are long strings of information (phone numbers, account numbers, dates) formatted into readable chunks? Are step-by-step processes divided into logical sections? Are form fields grouped by topic? Are list items grouped with section headers?

**Law 11 — Hick's Law (hicks-law)**: Measure the number of choices at key decision points. Are navigation menus organized hierarchically to reduce choice at each level? Are there smart defaults that reduce the number of decisions? Are filters and sorting available to narrow options? Calculate approximate decision complexity at critical interaction points.

**Law 12 — Selective Attention (selective-attention)**: Do critical actions have sufficient visual prominence? Are there distracting elements competing for attention during key tasks (ads, notifications, unrelated content)? Is the visual hierarchy consistent with task priority? Are primary actions clearly distinguished from secondary actions?

**Law 13 — Mental Model (mental-model)**: Does the interface organization match how users think about the domain? Are workflows consistent with real-world processes? Do icons and metaphors align with user expectations? Does terminology use the user's vocabulary, not system jargon? Are there conceptual model mismatches that could cause confusion?

For each law, assign a compliance score (0-4), document findings with exact references, and tag each finding.

### Phase 4: Behavioral Laws Evaluation
Evaluate motivation, memory formation, and effort allocation against the 9 behavioral laws:

**Law 14 — Goal-Gradient Effect (goal-gradient)**: Do multi-step processes show progress indicators? Does the interface communicate proximity to completion? Are progress bars or step counters present in onboarding, checkout, and setup flows? Is progress visually proportional to actual completion?

**Law 15 — Zeigarnik Effect (zeigarnik)**: Does the interface leverage incomplete task awareness? Are draft states, partially completed profiles, or pending actions clearly indicated? Are users reminded of uncompleted tasks? Is the tension between completion and abandonment well-managed (not anxiety-inducing)?

**Law 16 — Peak-End Rule (peak-end)**: What is the peak emotional moment of the primary user flow? Is it designed to be positive? What is the final moment of each major flow (confirmation screens, success messages, exit points)? Are error experiences (potential negative peaks) mitigated? Are success celebrations appropriate and satisfying?

**Law 17 — Flow State (flow)**: Is the challenge-skill balance appropriate? Are goals clear at each step? Is feedback immediate and unambiguous? Are interruptions (modals, alerts, loading states) minimized during focused tasks? Does the interface support sustained engagement without frustration?

**Law 18 — Paradox of the Active User (paradox-active-user)**: Can users jump into tasks without reading instructions first? Does the interface support learning by doing? Are help systems contextual rather than requiring upfront reading? Is error recovery graceful (users can explore without fear of irreversible mistakes)?

**Law 19 — Pareto Principle (pareto)**: Are the most-used 20% of features prominently accessible? Are rarely-used features appropriately de-emphasized? Does the interface prioritize common tasks over edge cases? Is the information architecture weighted by usage frequency?

**Law 20 — Parkinson's Law (parkinsons-law)**: Do open-ended tasks have appropriate timeframes or deadlines? Are session timeouts and auto-saves implemented for long processes? Does the interface encourage efficient completion rather than open-ended browsing where task completion matters?

**Law 21 — Von Restorff Effect (von-restorff)**: Are critical elements (CTAs, warnings, status changes) visually distinct from surrounding content? Is visual emphasis used strategically (not everything is emphasized)? Are new features, unread items, or urgent actions differentiated? Does the design avoid 'emphasis fatigue' where too many things compete for attention?

**Law 22 — Serial Position Effect (serial-position)**: Are the most important items placed at the beginning or end of lists, menus, and navigation? Are middle positions reserved for less critical items? Do navigation structures leverage primacy and recency for key destinations? Are dropdown and select menus ordered with important options at extremes?

For each law, assign a compliance score (0-4), document findings, and tag each finding.

### Phase 5: Design Principles Evaluation
Evaluate interaction design quality against the 8 design principles:

**Law 23 — Jakob's Law (jakobs-law)**: Does the interface follow platform conventions (iOS HIG, Material Design, web standards)? Are common patterns (navigation placement, search behavior, form submission, error display) consistent with what users encounter on other sites/apps? Are there any 'creative' deviations from convention that sacrifice learnability?

**Law 24 — Fitts's Law (fitts-law)**: Are primary action buttons large enough for easy targeting? Are frequently-used controls placed near likely cursor/finger positions? Are touch targets at least 44x44 CSS pixels (per Apple HIG) or 48x48dp (per Material Design)? Are destructive actions placed away from common actions to prevent accidental activation? Are clickable areas generous (not just the text/icon)?

**Law 25 — Doherty Threshold (doherty-threshold)**: Are system responses under 400ms for common interactions? Are loading states provided for operations exceeding 400ms? Are skeleton screens or optimistic updates used for perceived performance? Are there any interactions that feel 'laggy' or unresponsive? Does the system use progressive loading for heavy content?

**Law 26 — Tesler's Law (teslers-law)**: Has the interface absorbed appropriate complexity on behalf of users? Are smart defaults, auto-detection, and pre-filled values used where possible? Are there areas where users are forced to make decisions the system could handle? Is complexity appropriately distributed between the system and the user?

**Law 27 — Occam's Razor (occams-razor)**: Is the interface free of unnecessary elements, steps, and decoration? Are there features or options that could be removed without loss? Is each screen element justified by a clear purpose? Does the design avoid over-engineering simple interactions?

**Law 28 — Postel's Law (postels-law)**: Does the system accept varied input formats (dates, phone numbers, search queries)? Are input validations forgiving (accepting 'Yes', 'yes', 'Y', 'y')? Are error messages helpful when input is rejected? Does the system auto-correct obvious input errors where safe to do so?

**Law 29 — Choice Overload (choice-overload)**: Are there any screens with an overwhelming number of options? Are recommendation systems, filters, or guided paths used to reduce choice burden? Are defaults provided for complex configuration? Do category pages avoid overwhelming users with product/option grids?

**Law 30 — Cognitive Bias Awareness (cognitive-bias)**: Does the interface avoid dark patterns that exploit cognitive biases? Are pricing and comparison displays fair and transparent? Are default selections ethical? Does the design avoid manipulative urgency, artificial scarcity, or confirmshaming? Are opt-in/opt-out patterns designed ethically?

For each law, assign a compliance score (0-4), document findings, and tag each finding.

### Phase 6: Nielsen Heuristic Cross-Reference
Map all identified violations to Nielsen's H1-H10 heuristics to create a bidirectional reference:
- For each violation found in Phases 2-5, identify which Nielsen heuristic(s) it also violates
- Use this mapping:
  - H1 (Visibility) <-> Doherty Threshold, Goal-Gradient Effect, Zeigarnik Effect
  - H2 (Real World Match) <-> Jakob's Law, Mental Model
  - H3 (User Control) <-> Postel's Law, Tesler's Law
  - H4 (Consistency) <-> Jakob's Law, Law of Similarity
  - H5 (Error Prevention) <-> Cognitive Load, Cognitive Bias, Postel's Law
  - H6 (Recognition) <-> Miller's Law, Working Memory, Chunking, Serial Position Effect
  - H7 (Flexibility) <-> Pareto Principle, Paradox of Active User, Fitts's Law
  - H8 (Aesthetic) <-> Occam's Razor, Law of Pragnanz, Aesthetic-Usability Effect
  - H9 (Error Recovery) <-> Mental Model, Peak-End Rule
  - H10 (Help) <-> Paradox of Active User, Chunking
- Document the mapping for each finding
- This cross-reference enables teams that use Nielsen's heuristics to understand the psychological basis for violations

### Phase 7: Compliance Scoring and Evidence Chain Construction

**Per-Law Scoring**: For each of the 30 laws, compute a compliance score (0-100) based on: number of violations found, their severity (0-4), weighted by frequency and impact. A law with zero violations scores 100. Each severity-4 violation reduces the law's score by 25 points; severity-3 by 15; severity-2 by 10; severity-1 by 5.

**Per-Cluster Scoring**: Calculate the average compliance score for each of the four clusters: Perception (laws 1-6), Cognitive (laws 7-13), Behavioral (laws 14-22), Principles (laws 23-30). Weight each law equally within its cluster.

**Overall Compliance Score**: Calculate as a weighted average of cluster scores: Perception (20%), Cognitive (30%), Behavioral (25%), Principles (25%). The weighting reflects the relative impact on usability.

**Radar Chart Data**: Produce data for a radar chart with 4 category axes (Perception, Cognitive, Behavioral, Principles) and optionally 30 detailed axes (one per law) for a detailed compliance profile.

**Evidence Chain Construction**:
- **Nuggets**: Atomic observations tied to specific laws and locations. CRITICAL: For each finding, include the tag 'ux-law:{law-id}' in the nugget's tags array, where {law-id} is the kebab-case identifier (e.g., 'ux-law:proximity', 'ux-law:millers-law', 'ux-law:fitts-law', 'ux-law:peak-end'). This tagging is mandatory for every nugget.
- **Facts**: Consolidated findings grouping related nuggets. Each fact identifies which laws are affected.
- **Insights**: Interpretive patterns explaining root causes. Each insight is supported by multiple facts.
- **Recommendations**: Specific design changes with priority, effort, laws addressed, and references to established design patterns.

Prioritize recommendations by: severity of law violations addressed, number of laws improved, implementation effort, and cross-reference to Nielsen heuristic violations.

## RULES FOR EVALUATION
1. Every violation must reference a specific screen/location and the specific law violated
2. Be specific: not 'too many options' but 'the settings page presents 23 ungrouped toggles, violating Miller's Law (7+/-2 limit) and Choice Overload'
3. Include positive findings for each law — what the interface does well
4. Distinguish between definite violations and potential violations requiring user testing to confirm
5. Do not double-count: if one design problem violates multiple laws, report it once with all applicable law references
6. Severity ratings must be justified with reasoning
7. Every nugget MUST include the 'ux-law:{law-id}' tag in its tags array — this is non-negotiable for traceability
8. Every recommendation must be tied to specific violations through the evidence chain

## OUTPUT QUALITY CRITERIA
- Every law must have at least an evaluation note (even if 'fully compliant')
- Critical violations (severity 3-4) must have detailed descriptions with exact location references
- The overall score must reflect the weighted severity distribution
- The Nielsen cross-reference must be complete for all violations
- Recommendations must be specific and implementable, with design pattern references
- The summary must communicate the top 3-5 priorities to a non-specialist audience

metadata.author: Istara
references: Yablonski, J. (2024). Laws of UX: Using Psychology to Design Better Products & Services (2nd ed.). O'Reilly.
lawsofux.com — Laws of UX reference. Licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0.
Wertheimer, M. (1923). Laws of Organization in Perceptual Forms. Psychologische Forschung, 4.
Koffka, K. (1935). Principles of Gestalt Psychology. Harcourt Brace.
Miller, G.A. (1956). The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two. Psychological Review, 63(2).
Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive Load During Problem Solving. Cognitive Science, 12(2).
Hick, W.E. (1952). On the Rate of Gain of Information. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 4(1).
Fitts, P.M. (1954). The Information Capacity of the Human Motor System. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 47(6).
Kahneman, D., Fredrickson, B.L., Schreiber, C.A. & Redelmeier, D.A. (1993). When More Pain Is Preferred to Less. Psychological Science, 4(6).
Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience. Harper & Row.
Tversky, A. & Kahneman, D. (1974). Judgment Under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases. Science, 185(4157).
Tractinsky, N., Katz, A.S. & Ikar, D. (2000). What Is Beautiful Is Usable. Interacting with Computers, 13(2).
Iyengar, S.S. & Lepper, M.R. (2000). When Choice Is Demotivating. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 79(6).
Carroll, J.M. & Rosson, M.B. (1987). Paradox of the Active User. In Interfacing Thought. MIT Press.
Nielsen, J. (1994). Enhancing the Explanatory Power of Usability Heuristics. CHI '94 Proceedings.
Nielsen, J. (2000). Jakob's Law of the Web User Experience. Nielsen Norman Group.
Doherty, W.J. & Kelisky, R.P. (1979). Managing VM/CMS Systems for User Effectiveness. IBM Systems Journal, 18(1).
Johnson-Laird, P.N. (1983). Mental Models. Harvard University Press.
Murdock, B.B. (1962). The Serial Position Effect of Free Recall. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 64(5).
Von Restorff, H. (1933). Analyse von Vorgangen im Spurenfeld. Psychologische Forschung, 18.
Zeigarnik, B.V. (1927). On Finished and Unfinished Tasks. Psychologische Forschung, 9.
Hull, C.L. (1932). The Goal-Gradient Hypothesis and Maze Learning. Psychological Review, 39(1).
Broadbent, D.E. (1958). Perception and Communication. Pergamon Press.
Cockton, G. & Woolrych, A. (2001). Understanding Inspection Methods. In People and Computers XV.

changelog.changes: Initial creation: comprehensive UX Law Compliance Audit skill covering all 30 Laws of UX (Yablonski, 2024) organized into 4 clusters (Perception, Cognitive, Behavioral, Principles). Includes 7-phase evaluation methodology, Nielsen heuristic cross-reference mapping, compliance scoring with radar chart data, and mandatory ux-law:{id} tagging for evidence chain traceability.
